items.name: Matched grip & the fulcrum — the pivot everything hangs off
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: The stick needs one 'hinge point' to pivot around, called the fulcrum. It sits about a third of the way up from the butt end, pinched lightly between the pad of the thumb and the first/second joint of the index finger — commonly taught as making a loose 'OK' sign around the stick (Yamaha, Drumeo). Find it by letting the stick balance and swing like a pendulum at that point before closing the rest of the hand around it. Matched grip = both hands use the identical hold, which is why it's the default starting grip for practice-pad learners (Yamaha Music, Wikipedia 'Grip (percussion)'). TOP MISTAKES: (1) Fulcrum too close to the butt end — loses control of the tip, stick feels 'floppy' and wanders off the pad. (2) Fulcrum too close to the tip — kills leverage and rebound, feels like you're 'muscling' every stroke and volume caps out low (Drumming.com/beginner guide; upbeat.studio). SELF-DIAGNOSIS: If you can't find a natural bounce-point where the stick rebounds on its own with a light bounce test, or if your hand cramps within a couple minutes, the fulcrum is misplaced or squeezed. CORRECTIVE CUE/EXERCISE: 'Pendulum test' — hold the stick only at the suspected fulcrum point with thumb+index, let it hang, then tap it lightly on the pad; a correctly placed, correctly loose fulcrum lets the stick rebound 2-3 times on its own before settling. Do this at the start of every session as a grip-check ritual.

items.name: Back fingers as the 'soft cage' — supporting rebound, not blocking it
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: The middle, ring, and pinky fingers wrap around the stick to guide and stabilize it, but they should act like a soft cage that follows the stick's natural bounce rather than a clamp that stops it (houseofdrumming.com; upbeat.studio 'hold like a baby bird — tight enough it doesn't fly away, loose enough you don't crush it'). Space check: you should be able to fit roughly two fingers from your other hand into the gap between thumb and index finger at rest. TOP MISTAKES: (1) Back fingers locked rigid against the stick at all times, which absorbs the rebound the stick would otherwise give back for free. (2) Back fingers doing nothing at all (all control from wrist), which loses the fine articulation needed for fast/quiet passages and for doubles later. HOW IT FEELS/SOUNDS: Rigid-finger mistake feels like every stroke requires a full new 'throw' from the wrist — arms tire fast, and the second partial-bounce of a double stroke sounds weak/uneven because the fingers choke it before it can speak. CORRECTIVE CUE/EXERCISE: 'Uncontrolled bounce' drill — drop the stick onto the pad from a few inches and let it bounce freely 4-6 times without any hand input, just watching how it decays naturally; then re-grip and try to reproduce that same free-decay feel on a single stroke, letting the back fingers relax open on the rebound instead of catching it dead.

items.name: Grip tension ('death grip' / choking the stick) — THE umbrella beginner failure mode
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: Nearly every beginner problem (weak rebound, fast fatigue, capped volume, dead tone) traces back to gripping too tight. The target is a 'soft clamp' — firm enough to steer the stick, light enough that it can bounce (multiple sources converge on this exact framing: drumhelper.com, tuneluma.com, candccustomdrums.com). TOP MISTAKES: squeezing harder as tempo/nerves increase — the opposite of what's needed, since speed requires MORE relaxation not less. HOW IT FEELS/SOUNDS — the concrete, self-diagnosable signs: (1) SOUND: the drum/pad tone goes 'dead' or 'choked' instead of ringing/full, because a clamped stick can't vibrate freely against the head. (2) BOUNCE: the stick bounces once and stops dead, like dribbling on carpet, instead of rebounding cleanly. (3) PHYSICAL: blisters forming in the middle of the palm or on the thumb within ~20 minutes of playing is a direct tell of overgripping; hand/forearm fatigue within 5 minutes is another. CORRECTIVE CUE/EXERCISE: Deliberately play a passage as quietly as possible while staying controlled — quiet playing is impossible with a death grip and forces the hand to loosen, building sensitivity (drumhelper.com quiet-practice tip). This should be a recurring watchOut check embedded across every early lesson, not a one-time lesson.

items.name: Wrist vs. fingers vs. arm — the motion continuum, not a single 'correct' joint
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: There is no single joint that does 'the' stroke — technique blends wrist, fingers, and (for big/loud strokes) forearm depending on tempo and dynamic. General rule of thumb from teaching literature: wrist gives the most control and is the default driver at slow-to-moderate tempo and normal dynamics; fingers are smaller/faster muscles that take over as tempo rises past what the wrist alone can cleanly repeat; forearm/arm adds power for loud accents and big strokes (drummingtips.com, freddycharlesmusic.com 'The Art of Finger Control'). A commonly corrected misconception: 'turn your wrist like a doorknob' is a useful FEELING cue, but the rotation anatomically originates higher in the forearm, not the wrist joint itself (drummagazine.com 'Anatomy of Drumming: It's Not All in the Wrist') — worth knowing so the learner doesn't over-isolate an anatomically wrong joint. TOP MISTAKES: (1) Using only the wrist at all tempos/dynamics, hitting a hard speed ceiling and needing to muscle through fast passages. (2) Playing 'sideways' (palms facing each other) instead of palms-down, which strains the wrist and blocks natural rotation (drummagazine.com). HOW IT FEELS: sideways/wrist-locked playing feels tight/effortful and tires the forearm quickly even at moderate tempo; correct blended technique feels like effort DECREASES as tempo increases (counterintuitive but the standard teaching point) because fingers take over the small fast motions. CORRECTIVE CUE: practice the same simple pattern (single strokes) across a slow-to-fast tempo ramp on a metronome, consciously noticing where the wrist alone starts to feel strained and letting fingers 'catch' the stick at that transition point rather than forcing the wrist harder.

items.name: The free stroke — the pad-first bread-and-butter stroke
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: The free stroke starts and ends high, thrown DOWN toward the pad and allowed to rebound back up to the starting height entirely on its own — you never actively lift or pull the stick back up (Bill Bachman via drumworkout.com/Modern Drummer teaching, echoed by Not So Modern Drummer's 'Different Strokes' series and virtualdrumming.com). It's the default stroke for building rebound feel because it makes the stick's natural bounce do most of the work — 'you only throw it down, like dribbling a ball' (Bachman). This should be the FIRST stroke taught pad-first, before down/up/tap variants, because everything else is a variation on trusting or interrupting this rebound. TOP MISTAKES: (1) Actively lifting the stick back up after the hit instead of letting rebound carry it — defeats the entire purpose and re-introduces tension. (2) Stopping the stick's descent partway or 'placing' it on the head rather than throwing it. HOW IT FEELS/SOUNDS: a correct free stroke feels almost like you let go of control for a split second after the throw; an incorrect one feels like constant micromanagement of the stick on every single note, and sounds inconsistent in volume because the player is manually re-deciding stick height each time instead of trusting a repeatable throw. CONCRETE OBSERVABLE CRITERION for a watchOut/goodWhen field: 'stick rebounds back to within about an inch of its starting height without you lifting it' — if it doesn't get most of the way back on its own, the throw or the grip tension needs adjustment. CORRECTIVE EXERCISE: single strokes at a slow tempo, deliberately pausing half a second after each stroke to consciously observe whether the stick already returned near full height before the next throw.

items.name: The full stroke — the same rebound idea, played through a whole phrase
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: The full stroke starts high, strikes, and ends high again (high-to-high) — functionally the free stroke concept applied as the general-purpose stroke used from moderate through loud dynamics (blackswamp.com 'The 4 Essential Snare Drum Strokes'; PAS official stroke-type definitions, pas.org). It's initiated by a combination of finger and wrist motion, not either alone. TOP MISTAKE: treating every note as a full stroke regardless of the musical context (accents vs. taps), which flattens dynamics and makes patterns sound robotic — a common beginner tell when they haven't yet been introduced to down/up/tap as DISTINCT tools. HOW IT SOUNDS: a set of notes played all as full strokes when some should be quiet taps sounds evenly loud/mechanical with no dynamic shape. CORRECTIVE CUE: introduce full stroke only after free stroke is solid, and immediately contrast it against the tap stroke (small, low-to-low) so the learner hears the dynamic difference rather than defaulting to one stroke size for everything.

items.name: The down stroke — the accent-then-control stroke
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: The down stroke starts high and ends LOW (high-to-low) — it's the accented stroke, and critically, it's what sets up quiet playing immediately afterward. It's initiated by snapping the back fingers closed at the moment of impact, which stops the stick low rather than letting it rebound back up (blackswamp.com; PAS stroke definitions). This is the one stroke that DELIBERATELY overrides free-stroke rebound — worth flagging explicitly to a self-learner so they don't think they're 'doing free stroke wrong.' TOP MISTAKE: letting the stick rebound back up after an accent when the next note needs to be quiet — this is the single most common cause of an accent 'bleeding' into the following note at the wrong volume. HOW IT FEELS: a correct down stroke feels like a deliberate 'catch' with the back fingers right at the bottom of the stroke, distinct from the passive/relaxed feel of a free stroke. CORRECTIVE EXERCISE: play accent-then-tap pairs (loud, quiet, loud, quiet) and check by ear whether the quiet note after the accent is actually quiet — if it's still loud, the down stroke wasn't caught and rebounded instead.

items.name: The up stroke — the preparatory stroke, not a hit in itself
items.detail: PLAIN EXPLANATION: The up stroke starts low and ends high (low-to-high) — it's essentially a quiet tap stroke that finishes in the raised position, existing solely to set up the NEXT stroke (usually a full or down stroke) at the correct height (blackswamp.com; PAS). Its entire purpose is preparation, not sound production. TOP MISTAKE: treating the up stroke as unimportant/skippable since it's quiet, which leaves the stick at the wrong height for the next accent and forces a rushed, uneven-sounding follow-up stroke. HOW IT SOUNDS: sequences with a missed or sloppy up stroke sound like the accent right after it is either late or under-powered, because the stick had to travel further than planned in less time. CORRECTIVE CUE: practice up-stroke-into-down-stroke (quiet-then-loud) pairs in isolation, checking that the up stroke actually reaches full height before the accent begins — this pairing is the foundation for playing any dynamic accent pattern cleanly, which matters even on a pad since dynamics/accents are audible on rubber.

items.name: Moeller technique — teach LATER, not in v1 (with the reasoning)
items.detail: VERDICT FOR CURRICULUM: Do not introduce Moeller in the pad-first v1 curriculum; flag it as a 'horizon 2' technique once free/full/down/up strokes and basic rebound control are solid. PLAIN EXPLANATION (for when it IS introduced later): Moeller is a whipping/wave motion that combines multiple strokes into one arm motion, using gravity so a single downward whip generates one stroke on the way down and a second rebound stroke on the way up, gaining speed and power with less muscular effort (Wikipedia 'Moeller method'; Melodics; drumhelper.com). WHY NOT YET: multiple teaching sources converge that Moeller has a genuinely steep learning curve and beginners who attempt it before basic stroke control is automatic tend to develop bad habits by relying on the whip's efficiency as a crutch rather than building fundamentals (kickstartyourdrumming.com; electronicdrumadvisor.com). It also assumes the free-stroke rebound concept is ALREADY second nature — Moeller is explicitly built on top of the four basic strokes (full/down/up/tap), so sequencing it before those are solid inverts the correct learning order (electronicdrumadvisor.com: 'the Moeller Method uses the whipping motion and applies it to the 4 basic strokes'). GATING CRITERION for when to introduce it later: learner can play free strokes with visible/audible consistent rebound AND can execute clean down/up stroke pairs at a moderate tempo without re-gripping between notes.

items.name: Self-diagnosis toolkit — how a solo pad-only learner checks their own technique without a teacher
items.detail: Because this learner has no teacher and no kit (pad only, per the brief), watchOut fields need OBSERVABLE, self-checkable criteria rather than 'feel right' language. Concrete checks gathered across sources: (1) SOUND CHECK — pad/drum tone should sound open and 'live', not dead/choked; a choked sound is the single clearest tension signal (drumhelper.com, tuneluma.com). (2) BOUNCE CHECK — after a single free stroke, the stick should rebound most of the way back to start height on its own; one dead bounce (like dribbling on carpet) = overgripping. (3) BLISTER/FATIGUE CHECK — blisters in the mid-palm/thumb within ~20 minutes, or forearm fatigue within ~5 minutes, both indicate excess grip tension, not 'building strength' (a common beginner misread — fatigue this fast is a technique bug, not a fitness gap). (4) MIRROR/SYMMETRY CHECK — since there's no teacher to eyeball hand position, prop a phone/mirror and compare left vs right hand height and motion on alternating single strokes; asymmetry between hands is invisible by feel but obvious by sight (thenonglamorousdrummer.com style guidance, general pad-practice sources). (5) QUIET-PLAYAY CHECK — if the learner cannot play a passage quietly and controlled, grip tension is almost certainly the cause, since a death grip makes quiet playing physically difficult; deliberately practicing at low volume is both a diagnostic AND a corrective exercise. These five checks are cheap enough to run every session and should be embedded as explicit watchOut criteria rather than left implicit.

summary: Beginner drum technique on a practice pad collapses to one core skill — trusting stick rebound instead of muscling every note — taught through a specific sequence: matched grip with a correctly-placed, loosely-held fulcrum (thumb + index 'OK sign' about a third up the stick), back fingers acting as a soft supportive cage rather than a clamp, then the four PAS-standard stroke types (free/full/down/up), each with a distinct purpose and a distinct observable failure mode. Grip tension ('death grip'/'choking the stick') is the single umbrella beginner mistake underlying nearly everything else, and it is self-diagnosable without a teacher via sound (choked vs. open tone), bounce behavior (dead bounce vs. natural rebound), and fatigue speed (blisters/tiredness within minutes signal overgripping, not weak muscles). Wrist/fingers/arm are not competing techniques but a continuum — wrist dominates at slow-to-moderate tempo, fingers take over as speed increases, arm adds power for accents — and the popular 'turn like a doorknob' cue is anatomically loose (rotation is forearm-driven) but fine as a feel-cue. Moeller technique should explicitly be deferred past v1: it's built on top of already-automatic free/down/up strokes, and teaching literature repeatedly warns it becomes a crutch that cements bad habits if introduced before basic stroke control is solid. Because this learner is self-taught and pad-only, watchOut/goodWhen fields should lean on concrete, self-checkable criteria (e.g., 'stick rebounds to within an inch of start height without lifting,' 'quiet note after an accent is actually quiet,' 'no blisters within 20 minutes') rather than feel-based language a teacher would normally supply live.
